coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots/PtW6O24 composite as efficient and stable electrocatalyst for hydrogen oxidation reaction in PEMFCs
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yunjie
Yu, Feiyang
Lang, Zhongling
Nie, Haodong
Wang, Zhenzhen
Shao, Mingwang
Liu, Yang
Tan, Huaqiao
Li, Yangguang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Platinum (Pt) is practical available candidate for satisfying the requirements of anode catalysts in the proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs). Totally dispersed Pt is an ideal way to minimize Pt use, and the develop of Pt-single-atom based hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) catalysts should be carried out in a multi-component/-function model system with a clear active site (Pt-single-atom) geometric structure. This work combines the well-defined Na6[H2PtW6O24] clusters and multi-function carbon dots (CDs) to show the highly efficient Pt-single-atom catalyst design for HOR in acid. The mass activity and exchange current density of POMs/CDs (2.18 wt% Pt) achieved 86.72 A mgpt −1 and 3.78 A mgpt −1, respectively, which were 9.5 and 14 times that of 20% Pt/C. Also, this catalyst exhibits better performance than 20% Pt/C in the test of single cell, including higher open circuit potential (OCP), higher power density and better long-time stability. In particular, CDs can effectively improve the catalytic performance of POMs by enhancing the electron acquisition ability of Pt in POMs, the conductivity of composites, the structure stability, the ability to resist toxicity and accelerating the transfer of H+ produced on POMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyoxometalates
Low platinum material
Hydrogen oxidation reaction
PEMFCs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Studies on the mufti-functions of CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National Key Research and Development Project of China ( 2020YFA0406101 , 2020YFA0406104 ), National MCF Energy R&D Program ( 2018YFE0306105 ), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51821002 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51725204 , 21771132 , 51972216 , 52041202 , 22071020 , 21771033 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190041 ), Key-Area Research and Development Program of GuangDong Province ( 2019B010933001 ), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology , and the 111 Project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization by TEM. (A, B) TEM images of CDs (inset: HRTEM image and particle size distribution of CDs) and PtW6O24-CDs-4. (C, D) HRTEM images of PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4, where CDs are highlighted in red circles. (E-I) Corresponding elemental mapping images of C, O, Pt and W of PtW6O24-CDs-4.

Structure of POMs and POMs/CDs. (A) XRD patterns of CDs, and PtW6O24-CDs-4 and simulated XRD patterns from single crystal of PtW6O24 (inset: Digital photo of PtW6O24-CDs-4). (B, C, D) Raman spectra and FT-IR spectra of CDs, PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4. (E, F) XPS spectra of PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4: (E) Pt, (F) W.

HOR performance of catalysts. (A) CVs obtained for PtW6O24-CDs-4. (B) HOR polarization curves obtained on PtW6O24-CDs-4, PtW6O24, and commercial 20%Pt/C. (C) HOR polarization curves of PtW6O24-CDs-4 with different rotation speeds. (D) Koutecky-Levich plot of PtW6O24-CDs-4. (E) Kinetic polarization curves for the HOR (inset compares the micropolarization potential range for PtW6O24-CDs-4, PtW6O24 and 20% Pt/C). (F) Mass activity and mass-normalized exchange current densities of all samples obtained from the slope of the linear fitting in the micropolarization region.

Anti-carbon monoxide poisoning performance. HOR polarization curves of 20% Pt/C (A), PtW6O24 (B) and PtW6O24-CDs-4 (C) tested in H2 saturated (solid lines, red) and H2 mixed with 1% CO saturated (dash lines, blue) solutions. (D) Isotherm linear plot of CO for CDs and graphite.

Mechanisms of enhancement of electrocatalytic activity by CDs. (A) Comparison of the TPV curves and charge extraction processes of CDs, PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4. (B) Part of the CV in acetonitrile(Left), and tafel slopes (Right) of PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4. (C) Nyquist plot of CDs, PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4. (D) H+ adsorption capacity (mmol g−1) of CDs, PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4.

Electrocatalytic performance and in-situ XANES spectra of the PtMo6O24-CDs-4. (A) HOR polarization curves obtained on PtMo6O24-CDs-4, and PtMo6O24. (B) Mass activity and mass-normalized exchange current densities of PtMo6O24-CDs-4, and PtMo6O24. (C) In-situ XANES spectra of the PtMo6O24-CDs-4 at Pt L3-edge. (D) The fourier transform of the EXAFS data of PtMo6O24-CDs-4.

Performance of the PEMFC with 20% Pt/C and PtW6O24-CDs-4 as cathode catalysts: (A) Structure of PEMFC used in this work. (B) Open circuit potential. (C) Polarization curves. (D) Constant current (10 mA cm−2) discharges of the fuel cells.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots/PtW6O24 composite as efficient and stable electrocatalyst for hydrogen oxidation reaction in PEMFCs

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CDs/PtW6O24 composite with excellent catalytic activity for hydrogen oxidation reaction in acidic environment.

Platinum (Pt) is practical available candidate for satisfying the requirements of anode catalysts in the proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs). Totally dispersed Pt is an ideal way to minimize Pt use, and the develop of Pt-single-atom based hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) catalysts should be carried out in a multi-component/-function model system with a clear active site (Pt-single-atom) geometric structure. This work combines the well-defined Na6[H2PtW6O24] clusters and multi-function carbon dots (CDs) to show the highly efficient Pt-single-atom catalyst design for HOR in acid. The mass activity and exchange current density of POMs/CDs (2.18 wt% Pt) achieved 86.72 A mgpt −1 and 3.78 A mgpt −1, respectively, which were 9.5 and 14 times that of 20% Pt/C. Also, this catalyst exhibits better performance than 20% Pt/C in the test of single cell, including higher open circuit potential (OCP), higher power density and better long-time stability. In particular, CDs can effectively improve the catalytic performance of POMs by enhancing the electron acquisition ability of Pt in POMs, the conductivity of composites, the structure stability, the ability to resist toxicity and accelerating the transfer of H+ produced on POMs.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR), the anode reaction to convert H2 into electricity, is particularly appealing owing to available compact proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) [1–3]. Platinum (Pt) is generally considered as the practical available candidates for satisfying the requirement of anode catalysts in such fuel cells in acid [4]. The kinetics of HOR is very fast on Pt catalysts, and the oxidation of H2 at higher current densities is usually limited by mass transfer [5]. On the other hand, the performance of this fuel cell can be affected by the purity of H2, which comes from reformed natural gas or alcohols and often mixed with CO. It's worth noting that even low concentrations of CO can severely poison the Pt catalysts [6–9]. In addition, there is no doubt that the complete dispersion of Pt is an effective method to reduce the use of Pt, especially in the form of single atoms [10–12]. Thus, the design and development of high-efficient HOR catalyst should focus on reducing the use of Pt, improving the mass transfer rate at the active site, and improving resistance to CO poisoning of the catalyst. Besides, the development of Pt-single-atom based HOR catalysts should be better carried out in a multi-component/-function model system with a clear active site geometric structure, which is conductive to the rational design of catalyst and the study of catalytic mechanism.

Polyoxometalates (POMs) are unique class of metal-oxo clusters with transition metals in their highest oxidation states and can combine with almost all the transition metal elements and other main-group non-metal elements [13–16]. Therefore, POMs can be used as an ideal structure to realize the utilization of Pt at atomic level. In addition, POMs have excellent and strong electron acceptability, meaning that POMs can speed up the electron transfer in hydrogen decomposition [17–20]. In our previous work, We used two polyacid molecules of PtW6 and Pt2W10 in the electrocatalyst molecular model to prove that the Pt-O bond acts as the reaction center in the HER reaction, and its catalytic activity is much higher than 20 wt% Pt/C, and the reaction mechanism of Pt-O synergistic catalysis was also studied [13].

On the other hand, carbon dots (CDs, within 10 nm) known as nanocarbon with unique physical and chemical properties, have attracted considerable attention [21–22]. Especially in the field of catalysis, CDs have become a very attractive new material, due to their abundant surface functional groups, H+ adsorption and electron storage ability [23], high stability and specific surface area and efficient and stable reactive active center [24–27].

In this work, we report a POMs/CDs composite catalyst used for HOR in acidic environment. The complex was synthesized by concentrated crystallization, which is simple and gentle. The POMs/CDs with 2.18 wt% Pt content exhibits much higher catalytic performance than that of 20 wt% commercial platinum carbon. The mass activity of POMs/CDs reached 86.72 A mgpt −1 at 50 mV vs. RHE, which is 9.5 times higher than that of 20% Pt/C. Also, the exchange current density is 3.78 A mgpt −1, which is more than 14 times larger than commercial Pt/C. Furthermore, with the introduction of CDs, the catalytic performance and stability of POMs can also be greatly improved. Notably, CDs show significant effects on the improvement of catalytic performance, including the enhancement of the electronic ability of POMs, material conductivity, structural stability, the ability to resist CO poisoning and assisting the transfer of reaction products. In present work, the combine of Pt single-atom active site in polyanions with clear structure and muti-fuctional CDs enable us to creates new ideas and structural research models for the understanding and design of highly efficient and stable single-atom Pt based electrocatalysts for HOR.
Na2Pt(OH)6 (99.9%) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Na2WO4·2H2O (99.0%) and Na2MoO4·2H2O were purchased from Aladdin. HNO3 (AR, 98.0%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

CDs were prepared by electrolytic graphite rods as we have already reported [28]. The obtained CDs solution was centrifuged at 16,500 rpm for 30 min, and the upper liquid was retained. The concentration of CDs solution used in the work is 4 mg mL−1.

0.15 mmol Na2Pt(OH)6 and 0.9 mmol Na2WO4·2H2O were mixed in 30 mL ultrapure water, and dispersed by ultrasound until completely dissolved. 1 M HNO3 was used to adjust the pH to ca 6.2. Then the solution was heated at 80 ℃ for 4 h and concentrated to 5 mL. After three days, yellow crystals were obtained. As a result, the chemical formula of this crystals is Na6[H2PtW6O24]·22H2O (designated PtW6O24). Beyond that, different volumes (1 mL, 2 mL, 3 mL, 4 mL, 5 mL) of CDs were added to the solution in the first step, and the crystals obtained were designated PtW6O24-CDs-n (n = 1,2,3,4,5).

PtMo6O24 was synthesized according to the literature [29], 0.15 mmol Na2Pt(OH)6 and 0.9 mmol Na2MoO4·2H2O were used as raw material. In addition, 4 mL CDs were added during the synthesis, the crystals obtained were designated PtMo6O24-CDs-4.

The CDs were prepared by an electrochemical method as described before, while the Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CDs are shown in Fig. 1 A, which depicts that CDs are highly crystalline with a lattice spacing of about 0.21 nm, matching with the (100) plane of carbon. The size of CDs is about 5 nm and uniformly dispersed. The POMs (Na6[H2PtW6O24], designated PtW6O24) were synthesized according to the literature [30], and the TEM image of PtW6O24 can be seen in Figure S1. CDs were introduced into the growth process of the crystal. As shown in Fig. 1B, the TEM image of PtW6O24-CDs-4 shows that a large number of CDs particles were mixed into the PtW6O24. Comparing with the HRTEM image (Fig. 1C) of PtW6O24 with a lattice spacing of 0.36 nm assigned to its (2–2 −1) plane, the Fig. 1D and Figure S2 show a well-defined lattice spacing of 0.46 nm and 0.21 nm consistent with the (020) plane of PtW6O24 and (100) plane of CDs, respectively, indicating that CDs are evenly dispersed in the structure of PtW6O24. A high-angle anular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image and the corresponding element mapping (Fig. 1E-I) further indicate the uniform distribution of carbon of CDs in the PtW6O24.

As depicted in Figure S3, the [H2PtW6O24]6− polyanion has an ideal Anderson-type structure with point symmetry D3d, and the crystal data of PtW6O24 are shown in Table S1. Pt atom is located in the centre of the polyanion and six W atoms are located at the corners of the regular hexagon. All the Pt and W atoms are connected by O atoms, and form regular octahedral structures ({PtO6}, {WO6}) in an edge-sharing mode. The length of Pt-O bond is about 2.01 Å, and the average bond lengths of W = O, W-O-W and W-O-Pt are about 1.75, 1.93, 2.21 Å, respectively. Fig. 2 A shows the powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of PtW6O24-CDs-4, which is consistent with the simulate XRD pattern from single crystal of PtW6O24, and show the diffraction peak of CDs. The image inserted in Fig. 2A is the digital photo of PtW6O24-CDs-4, which appears as crystals with black sheen. The Raman spectra (Fig. 2B) also show that PtW6O24-CDs-4 has both D (1325.2 cm−1) and G (1584.0 cm−1) bands from CDs [31,32], and the peaks at 819.4 cm−1, 870.9 cm−1, 947.1 cm−1 can be attributed to PtW6O24 [33]. Fig. 2C-D show the FT-IR spectra of CDs, PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4. The absorption peaks of PtW6O24-CDs-4 at 953.5 cm−1, 902.3 cm−1, 673.1 cm−1, 470.5 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of W-O, W-O-W, W-O-Pt and Pt-O bonds, respectively, which derived from {WO6} and {PtO6}. It is worth noting that all these absorption peaks shift to higher wavenumbers with the introduction of CDs, indicating that W-O, W-O-W, W-O-Pt and Pt-O bonds get strengthened which is an obvious outcome. The blueshift shows that CDs can make the structure of PtW6O24 more stable [34]. In addition, the spectrum of PtW6O24-CDs-4 has extra peaks located at 1711.7 cm−1 and 1234.2 cm−1 derived from the C = O bond and C-O-C bond of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to examine the chemical state of the PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4 catalyst. The XPS spectra of CDs are shown in Figure S4. And Figure S5 presents the full spectrum of the PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4, which demonstrates the presence of Pt, W, Na, O and C species. Fig. 2E provides the high-resolution XPS spectra of Pt 4f, PtW6O24 has two peaks at 78.16 eV and 74.79 eV attributed to the 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 orbitals of Pt(IV) species [35,36], respectively. The binding energy of Pt 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 of PtW6O24-CDs-4 display a positive shift of ~0.31 eV as compared to that of PtW6O24. Besides, Fig. 2F also shows that the binding energy of W 4f5/2 (37.58 eV) and 4f7/2 (35.39 eV) of PtW6O24-CDs-4 are about 0.15 eV higher than that of PtW6O24 (37.43 eV, 35.25 eV) [37]. These results indicate that the CDs can effectively regulate the electronic structure of POMs, which can make the charge shift from POMs to CDs. These experiments demonstrate that PtW6O24-CDs can be used as a platinum oxide model for the electrochemical study of HOR.

The HOR activities of samples were examined with a standard three-electrode system in H2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 solution at a scan rate of 5 mV·s−1 (See details in Methods). Fig. 3 A shows a comparison of cyclic voltammograms (CVs) in H2 vs N2 saturated electrolyte, confirming the activity of PtW6O24-CDs-4 towards the HOR. Specifically, the polarization curves registered in H2-saturated electrolyte for 20% Pt/C, PtW6O24-CDs-4 and PtW6O24 were assessed on a rotating disk electrode in 0.1 M HClO4 (Fig. 3B). The PtW6O24-CDs-4 sample has a much higher current density (2.63 mA cmgeom −2 at 0.1 V vs. RHE) than PtW6O24 (2.41 mA cmgeom −2), even higher than 20% Pt/C (2.47 mA cmgeom −2). The electrochemical surface areas (ECSAs) were tested by CV curves in 0.1 M HClO4 as shown in Figure S6. The ECSA of PtW6O24-CDs-4 was calculated to be 37.7 m2 gPt −1, which was almost the same as PtW6O24 (37.3 m2 gPt −1). Both the ECSAs for PtW6O24-CDs-4 and PtW6O24 are less than that of 20% Pt/C (63.2 m2 gPt −1), probably because the Pt atoms in the POMs were surrounded by O atoms. The performance of catalysts with different content of CDs is shown in Figure S7, the limiting diffusion current density increased with the the increase of the content of CDs, and reached the maximum value when the amount of CDs was 4 mL. When the amount of CDs reaches 5 mL, the current density drops, which may be due to the decrease of the overall platinum content proportion. Fig. 3C shows the dependence of the HOR activity as a function of potential and rotation rate. The current increases rapidly with potential on each curve, reaching a limiting value at about 50 mV vs. RHE. In addition, the Levich- Koutecky plot indicates that the current at the positive potential limit is essentially diffusion limited for PtW6O24-CDs-4 [38]. And the slope of the kinetic polarization curve is 12.44 mA−1*cm2 geom*rpm1/2 as shown in Fig. 3D. The kinetic current of HOR obtained from RDE data is normalized by the amount of Pt on the electrode to give a mass activity (MA), which can be used to compare the intrinsic activity of those catalysts. The content of the platinum element of samples loaded on carbon was tested by Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS) as shown in Table S2. The MA of PtW6O24-CDs-4 (86.72 A mgpt −1) and PtW6O24 (60.71 A mgpt −1) are 9.5 and 6.7 times higher than that of 20% Pt/C (9.11 A mgpt −1), respectively. The kinetic currents (jk) of HOR deduced from Koutecky-Levich equation from the RDE curves are shown in Fig. 3E as a function of the electrode potential. The exchange current (j0 ) also can be used to compare the values of HOR activity, which can be calculated using the micropolarization approach in the range from 0 to 10 mV vs. RHE (inset Fig. 3E), where the Butler - Volmer equation [39,40]:
(1)
j k
=
j 0
n F η
RT

can be approximated to a linear equation in a narrow overpotential range. n is the exchange electron number. R is the ideal gas constant. T is the temperature of test (298.15 K). η is the applied overpotential, and F is the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1). Fig. 3F shows the resulting exchange current density values normalized to the Pt loading. The 20% Pt/C exhibits an exchange current density of 0.267 A mgpt −1, which is similar to other reported work [39]. By contrast, the PtW6O24-CDs-4 exhibits exceptionally high exchange current density of 3.78 A mgpt −1, which is 1.4 times as big as PtW6O24 (2.7 A mgpt −1) and is 14 times larger than that of 20% Pt/C. Thus, PtW6O24 has excellent electrocatalytic activity for HOR, and with the addition of CDs, the catalytic reaction rate can be greatly enhanced.

Considering the source of hydrogen, trace amounts of carbon monoxide (CO) may be present in the hydrogen (co-produced during the catalytic reforming process), which could rapidly poison the surface of catalytic and reduce the power of full cell. This passivation process is especially noticeable on the surface of Pt [41]. So the anti-carbon monoxide poisoning performance is also a non-negligible problem in evaluating the catalyst for HOR. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the presence of 1% CO in H2, the properties of 20% Pt/C have decreased significantly, because CO can be easily adsorbed on the surface of platinum atoms, resulting that the active sites were occupies, thus losing of catalytic activity. Fig. 4B shows that the PtW6O24 appears to be much less susceptible to CO than 20% Pt/C. Furthermore, with the combination of CDs and PtW6O24, the ability of resisting CO poisoning was obviously enhanced (Fig. 4C). On the one hand, Pt atoms in POMs are not directly exposed to the surface, but surrounded by six O atoms. So CO cannot directly combine with Pt atoms, making it have a certain degree of anti-toxic effect. On the other hand, the adsorption of CO by CDs was investigated. Fig. 4D shows the adsorption isotherm of CDs and graphite for CO at room temperature (298 K). It can be found that CDs also have a significant adsorption effect for CO compared with ordinary graphite, and the measured adsorption amount can reach 0.8872 cm3 g−1 STP. Therefore, the physical adsorption of CO by CDs can also contribute to the anti-CO poisoning of the composite materials. Meanwhile, as shown in Figure S8, in the 12 h current–time test at 50 mV and 1600 rpm, with the increasing of amount of CDs, the stability of the complexes can be significantly improved, indicating that the CDs also play a positive role in improving stability of the catalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the mufti-functions of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The transient photovoltage (TPV) experiments were carried out to study the charge transfer ability of different components of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 5 A, CDs (tmax1) have a faster charge extraction process than that of PtW6O24 (tmax3), and the charge extraction rate of the PtW6O24-CDs-4 (tmax2) was significantly improved compared with that of individual PtW6O24, indicating that CDs have the ability to increase the charge transfer rate of the complex [42–44]. Fig. 5B (Left) shows part of the CV curves (Left) and tafel slopes (Right) of PtW6O24 and PtW6O24-CDs-4 tested in no-aqueous acetonitrile (the complete CV curves are shown in Figure S9), which contains the range of potential for HOR. There are obvious reduction peaks in this range, which belong to the process of electron acquisition of Pt-O-W species [13]. In order to study the kinetics of reduction reaction on Pt-O-W species, Butler - Volmer formulation was used as [45,46]:
(2)
i = n F A

( 0 , t )
e
- α f ( E -

)

C
R
( 0 , t )

( 1 - α )
f
( E -
E
0
'
)
where E0′ is the standard potential. C0 is the concentration of reactants, and CR is the concentration of products. To eliminate the effect of diffusion, only the potential range of the electron obtained by Pt-O-W just occurred was studied. At this time, the concentration of products is relatively low, so the equation can be simplified as:
(3)
i = n F A
k
0
C
0
( 0 , t )
e
- α f ( E -

0

)

Take the natural logarithm on both sides:
(4)

and rearranged as:

where f = F/RT, so:

when the current (i) is the same, we can get:
(8)

As shown in Fig. 5B (Right), η PtW 6 O24-CDs-4 < η PtW6O24, so k0
PtW6O24-CDs-4 > k0
PtW6O24. As a result, the Pt-O-W species in PtW6O24-CDs-4 have a faster kinetic rate during the reduction process compared with that in PtW6O24, which indicates that the introduction of CDs could also improve the ability of Pt-O-W species to acquire electrons during the catalytic reaction. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopies shown in Fig. 5C suggest that CDs possess the lower charge transfer resistance than PtW6O24. CDs play an important role in effectively reducing the charge transfer resistance of PtW6O24-CDs-4, which is also verified by TPV experiments. As shown in Fig. 5D, the amount of H+ adsorbed on CDs increases rapidly in the first 20 min, reaching a maximum of ~72.2 mmol g−1. While the adsorption capacity of H+ on PtW6O24 is only ~10.26 mmol g−1. The strong proton adsorption capacity of CDs can timely transfer H+ produced on PtW6O24 to a certain extent, which means that the reaction rate can be accelerated by promoting the desorption of the reaction products. All these results indicate that the CDs have excellent ability to promote both charge transfer and proton transfer, which is significantly important for the processes of HOR.

POMs (PtMo6O24) with the same structure (Figure S10-11) as PtW6O24 were also synthesized, and the electrocatalytic properties were studied. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the PtMo6O24 also exhibits excellent catalytic activity for HOR, and the addition of CDs also has an obvious effect on the improvement of catalytic performance. the mass activity of PtMo6O24-CDs-4 and PtMo6O24 was calulated to be 84.2 A mgpt −1 and 59.27 A mgpt −1 (Fig. 6B, Figure S12), respectively, which are close to the performance of PtW6O24-CDs-4 and PtW6O24. At the same time, the exchange current density (j0 ) of PtMo6O24-CDs-4 and PtMo6O24 has also reached 3.73 A mgpt −1 and 2.57 A mgpt −1, respectively. The comparison of HOR performance of different samples in HClO4 can be seen in Table S3. Furthermore, in situ X-ray adsorption spectroscopy (XAS) was performed to observe the electronic state variation of Pt in POMs during the HOR process. As shown in Fig. 6C, the Pt of PtMo6O24-CDs-4 in initial state is similar to the Na2Pt(OH)6, but with the application of bias (50 mV vs. RHE), the intensity of Pt was significantly decreased and shifts towards lower energy, which is more matched with the Pt2+ of K2PtCl4, and there is no formation of Pt0 observed as shown in Fig. 6D. These data of in situ XAS show that Pt in POMs is first reduced to Pt2+ during the HOR process, which is also consistent with the reductiuon peak observed in the CV curves tested in no-aqueous acetonitrile. These results indicate that the Pt in oxidized state does have excellent catalytic activity for HOR, which provides a new idea for the design and development of HOR catalysts.

To further evaluate the actual performance of the catalyst in the PEMFCs, a simple single cell test device was constructed, as shown in Fig. 7 A. Membrane electrode assembly (MEA) is the core component of this fuel cell, consisting of two catalysts layers, gas diffusion layers and a proton exchange membrane. The working area of MEA we used is 1 cm2. Commercial Pt/C (0.4 mgpt cm−2) was used as the cathode catalyst. 20% Pt/C (0.1 mgpt cm−2) and PtW6O24-CDs-4 0.01 (mgpt cm−2) were used as the anode catalysts in the single cell, respectively. The single cell of PtW6O24-CDs-4 has an open circuit potential of 0.99 V, while the commercial Pt/C is 0.98 V (Fig. 7B). Fig. 7C shows that the single cell of PtW6O24-CDs-4 generates a peak power density of 140 mW cm−2 superior to that of 20% Pt/C (122 mW cm−2). Fig. 7D shows that the voltage of the single cell of 20% Pt/C as the anode catalyst declines from 0.8 V to 0.65 V after constant current discharges at 10 mA·cm−2. Meanwhile, the voltage of single cell constructed with PtW6O24-CDs-4 only declines from 0.8 V to 0.7 V after 12 h. It is suggested that the stability of PtW6O24-CDs-4 in fuel cell is better than that of commercial Pt/C. TEM images (Figure S13) before and after the test were compared and no significant difference was found, indicating that the structure of this carbon-loaded PtW6O24-CDs-4 is relatively stable. Furthermore, the XPS spectra of PtW6O24-CDs-4 dispersed in carbon before and after the single cell test are shown in Figure S14, while the high-resolution XPS spectra of Pt and W have no significant changes.

In summary, we report a POMs/CDs composite catalyst, combining the well-defined PtW6O24 and PtMo6O24 clusters and multi-function CDs, and show the highly efficient catalytic activity for HOR in acid. The catalytic performance of PtW6O24-CDs-4 with 2.18 wt% Pt content far exceeds that of 20 wt% Pt/C. It achieved 86.72 A mgpt −1 at 50 mV vs. RHE (reversible hydrogen electrode), which is 9.5 times higher than that of commercial Pt/C, and the exchange current density is 3.78 A mgpt −1, more than 14 times larger than commercial 20% Pt/C. This POMs/CDs composite catalyst also exhibited better performance than 20% Pt/C in the test of single cell, including higher open circuit potential, higher power density and better long-time stability. In this catalyst system, CDs were proven to enhance the electron acquisition ability of Pt in POMs, improve the conductivity and structure stability of composites, and accelerate the H+ transfer processes, which greatly promoting the mass transfer process during the HOR. Taken together, the introduction of CDs can increase the catalytic efficiency of POMs by about 40%. This work creates new ideas and structural research models for the understanding and design of highly efficient and stable single-atom Pt based electrocatalysts for HOR.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Kang, Phosphorus-doped porous carbon nitride for efficient sole production of hydrogen peroxide via photocatalytic water splitting with a two-channel pathway, J. Mater. Chem. A. 8 (2020) 3701–3707. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9TA13929J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Butler–Volmer–Monod model for describing bio-anode polarization curves

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.V.M. Hamelers, A. ter Heijne, N. Stein, R.A. Rozendal, C.J.N. Buisman, Butler–Volmer–Monod model for describing bio-anode polarization curves, Bioresour. Technol. 102 (2011) 381–387. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2010.06.156.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermodynamic derivation of a Butler-Volmer model for intercalation in Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Latz, J. Zausch, Thermodynamic derivation of a Butler–Volmer model for intercalation in Li-ion batteries, Electrochimica Acta. 110 (2013) 358–362. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2013.06.043.
